content.problemStatement:
Chef has a garden containing NNN cells. The iii-th cell has RiR_iRi​ red flowers and BiB_iBi​ blue flowers. Chef can collect only one type of flowers (either red or blue) from each cell.

Let XXX denote the total number of red flowers Chef collects and YYY denote the total number of blue flowers Chef collects. Chef wants to **maximize** the value of min(X,Y)\mathrm{min}(X, Y)min(X,Y). Can you help Chef?


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the number of cells in Chef's garden.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers R1,R2,…,RNR_1, R_2, \dots, R_NR1​,R2​,…,RN​ denoting the number of red flowers in each cell.
The third line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers B1,B2,…,BNB_1, B_2, \dots, B_NB1​,B2​,…,BN​ denoting the number of blue flowers in each cell.


Output Format
For each test case, output the maximum value of min(X,Y)\mathrm{min}(X, Y)min(X,Y).
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 100100100.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1:
If Chef collects 101101101 red flowers from the cell, Chef will have a total of 101101101 red flowers (X=101)(X = 101)(X=101) and 000 blue flowers (Y=0)(Y = 0)(Y=0). 
If Chef collects the 333 blue flowers from the cell, Chef will have a total of 000 red flowers (X=0)(X = 0)(X=0) and 333 blue flowers (Y=3)(Y = 3)(Y=3). 
Either way, min(X,Y)=0\mathrm{min}(X, Y) = 0min(X,Y)=0, the answer is 000.
Test case 2: Chef collects the 199199199 blue flowers from the first cell and the 200200200 red flowers from the second cell, so min(X,Y)=199\mathrm{min}(X, Y) = 199min(X,Y)=199, which is maximum.
Test case 3: Chef collects blue cells from the first three cells (Y=3+1+3)(Y = 3 + 1 + 3)(Y=3+1+3) and red flowers from the fourth cell (X=10)(X = 10)(X=10), so min(X,Y)=7\mathrm{min}(X, Y) = 7min(X,Y)=7, which is maximum.
